nativeRange.habitat: Slow-moving rivers and streams with dense vegetation and soft, acidic to neutral water

diet.notes: Accepts flake, pellet, and frozen foods readily. Has been documented eating small invertebrates, worms, brine shrimp, and plant matter in the wild. Benefits from variety including high-quality flake, frozen bloodworms, and occasional live foods.

imageCandidates.notes: Photo by Andre Karwath (Aka), 1000x533. Tack-sharp lone male showing the definitive iridescent blue-and-gold flank, orange lateral band, and extended caudal fin filaments — best diagnostic image.
Photo by 7TP (Krzysztof Bartosik), 6016x4000. High-res vivid male with purple-blue iridescence, orange dorsal band, and long filamentous fins in a bright planted tank; color/setting variety.
Photo by NasserHalaweh, 1728x1296. Two individuals together in a natural planted/blackwater setup — represents the shoaling species as kept.

summary: The Congo Tetra is a colorful African schooling fish from the Congo River basin. Males of the species (Phenacogrammus interruptus) develop iridescent blue, purple, and gold flanks along with extended fin filaments. Peaceful and best kept in groups of six or more in a spacious planted aquarium.
careNotes: The Congo Tetra (Phenacogrammus interruptus) belongs to the African tetra family Alestidae — a group distinct from the South American Characidae that most hobbyists associate with the word 'tetra'. Males are notably more colorful than females, showing iridescent purple, blue, and gold flanks with characteristic extended filaments on the dorsal, anal, and caudal fins; body length reaches up to 9 cm, and the fin extensions can reach 10 cm or more. Females tend to be silvery and smaller, around 6 cm. Groups of fewer than 6 fish tend to become shy and may not show their best coloration — a school of 6 or more is generally recommended. The Congo Tetra is sensitive to poor water quality; regular water changes and reliable filtration are advisable. It prefers subdued or diffuse lighting and appreciates a mix of open swimming space and planted areas. The species is not generally considered a fin-nipper, though minor fin-nipping has occasionally been reported, particularly in undersized groups or alongside very long-finned tankmates.
breedingNotes: The Congo Tetra is an egg-scatterer; the pair or group gradually deposits eggs across fine-leafed plants or a spawning mop over several hours. Spawning has been reported most readily in slightly acidic, soft water with good filtration and some morning light. Eggs are adhesive and typically hatch in approximately 6 days at optimal temperature. Breeding is considered moderately difficult — conditioning the breeders on live and frozen foods is generally advised before spawning attempts. Reported spawns can include 300 or more eggs. Fry are small and are typically started on infusoria or commercial fry foods for first feeding.

primary.name: FishBase
primary.notes: Primary source used in place of Aquarium Co-Op (no species page found) and SeriouslyFish (HTTP 520). Provides taxonomy, native range, water parameters, size, and behavioral data.

additional.name: Fishkeeping World
additional.notes: Cross-checked size (males 3.5 in / ~8.9 cm, females 2.5–2.75 in), temperature (73–82°F = 22.8–27.8°C), pH (6.0–8.0), hardness (3–18 dGH), tank size (20–30 gal), lifespan (3–5 years), diet, temperament, and grouping. Minor disagreement on tank minimum (20 gal / 76L vs. FishBase 100 cm tank length); biased toward 150L per playbook caution for an active schooling species.

fish.breedingNotes: The Congo Tetra is an egg-scatterer that gradually deposits eggs across fine-leafed plants or a spawning mop. Conditioning the breeders on live and frozen foods is generally advised before spawning attempts. Eggs typically hatch around 6 days after spawning.
